item.title: Single Hyperspectral Image Super-Resolution Using An Efficient Transformer Network
item.dc:creator: Haijun Wang
Yaowei Wang
Yalin Nie
Limei Huo
Tengfei Yang
Peiluan Li
item.description: Single hyperspectral image super-resolution aims to reconstruct the high-resolution counterpart of a hyperspectral scene from a single low-resolution input without external guidance. A major challenge lies in jointly modeling long-range spatial dependencies and complex spectral correlations under strict computational constraints. Standard Transformers suffer from quadratic complexity, making them impractical for high-dimensional data. Meanwhile, existing lightweight methods often exhibit limited receptive fields or decouple spatial-spectral modeling. To address these issues, we propose the Efficient Spatial-Spectral Transformer (ETNet). This architecture integrates global context modeling with local feature extraction. At its core is the Unified Spatial-Spectral Module (SSM), which captures global spatial context and inter-band correlations in a single block. We utilize the Spectral Correlation Coefficient (SCC) to guide affinity estimation, incorporating spectral priors into the attention mechanism. By adopting a kernelized formulation, ETNet achieves linear complexity while preserving a global receptive field. Furthermore, a channel attention submodule and a streamlined gated feed-forward network enhance representational efficiency. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ETNet achieves consistent gains over state-of-the-art methods in both quantitative performance and perceptual quality. Published: 2026-04-26T05:58:49+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.829 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Haijun Wang; Yaowei Wang; Yalin Nie; Limei Huo; Tengfei Yang; Peiluan Li

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104429

Relevance: 0.829 (must_read)

Single hyperspectral image super-resolution aims to reconstruct the high-resolution counterpart of a hyperspectral scene from a single low-resolution input without external guidance. A major challenge lies in jointly modeling long-range spatial dependencies and complex spectral correlations under strict computational constraints. Standard Transformers suffer from quadratic complexity, making them impractical for high-dimensional data. Meanwhile, existing lightweight methods often exhibit limited receptive fields or decouple spatial-spectral modeling. To address these issues, we propose the Efficient Spatial-Spectral Transformer (ETNet). This architecture integrates global context modeling with local feature extraction. At its core is the Unified Spatial-Spectral Module (SSM), which captures global spatial context and inter-band correlations in a single block. We utilize the Spectral Correlation Coefficient (SCC) to guide affinity estimation, incorporating spectral priors into the attention mechanism. By adopting a kernelized formulation, ETNet achieves linear complexity while preserving a global receptive field. Furthermore, a channel attention submodule and a streamlined gated feed-forward network enhance representational efficiency. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ETNet achieves consistent gains over state-of-the-art methods in both quantitative performance and perceptual quality.

item.title: Polarimetric second-moment response: A target characterization benchmark and its application in ship structure recognition
item.dc:creator: Junpeng Wang
Sinong Quan
Zhihao Cai
Shiqi Xing
Yongzhen Li
Wei Wang
Hao Wu
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Ship structure recognition using polarimetric information is of great significance for maritime remote sensing. However, accurate and reasonable ship structure recognition remains challenging, primarily due to the insufficiency of information exploration in target characterization space, and the lack of intelligent recognizer based on polarimetric-physical coupling. To address these limitations, we propose a novel Polarimetric Second-Moment Response (PSMR) and a polarimetric prior-based structure recognition framework. First, a three-dimensional polarization state vector (Q Vector) is derived, establishing a fundamental base for electromagnetic wave polarization state in complex space that simultaneously describes polarization orientation and ellipse angles and phase information. Second, for the first time, the PSMR concept is proposed, which provides a benchmark for target characterization. The PSMR spectrums are proposed to represents the polarimetric response, which reflects the polarimetric characteristics of each structure. And a designed average polarization similarity measure (APSM) quantifies the difference between the target and canonical structures by comparing their spectrums. Third, a polarimetric prior-based structure recognition framework PSAResNet is constructed, in which a polarimetric similarity attention (PSA) branch module and a similarity approximation loss function (SAL) are incorporated based on the APSM. The improved framework enhances the fusion of the neural network with polarimetric priors, enabling superior and more generalized performance of the structure recognition under measured environment. Experimental results on three measured PolSAR data of partial-coherent and distributed targets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves precise and physically consistent ship structure recognition. Furthermore, this study discusses the correlation between structure recognition and characteristics represented by model-based polarimetric decomposition. Published: 2026-04-25T04:09:05+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.810 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Junpeng Wang; Sinong Quan; Zhihao Cai; Shiqi Xing; Yongzhen Li; Wei Wang; Hao Wu

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.04.025

Relevance: 0.810 (must_read)

Ship structure recognition using polarimetric information is of great significance for maritime remote sensing. However, accurate and reasonable ship structure recognition remains challenging, primarily due to the insufficiency of information exploration in target characterization space, and the lack of intelligent recognizer based on polarimetric-physical coupling. To address these limitations, we propose a novel Polarimetric Second-Moment Response (PSMR) and a polarimetric prior-based structure recognition framework. First, a three-dimensional polarization state vector (Q Vector) is derived, establishing a fundamental base for electromagnetic wave polarization state in complex space that simultaneously describes polarization orientation and ellipse angles and phase information. Second, for the first time, the PSMR concept is proposed, which provides a benchmark for target characterization. The PSMR spectrums are proposed to represents the polarimetric response, which reflects the polarimetric characteristics of each structure. And a designed average polarization similarity measure (APSM) quantifies the difference between the target and canonical structures by comparing their spectrums. Third, a polarimetric prior-based structure recognition framework PSAResNet is constructed, in which a polarimetric similarity attention (PSA) branch module and a similarity approximation loss function (SAL) are incorporated based on the APSM. The improved framework enhances the fusion of the neural network with polarimetric priors, enabling superior and more generalized performance of the structure recognition under measured environment. Experimental results on three measured PolSAR data of partial-coherent and distributed targets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves precise and physically consistent ship structure recognition. Furthermore, this study discusses the correlation between structure recognition and characteristics represented by model-based polarimetric decomposition.

item.title: Heterogeneous Model Fusion for Privacy-Aware Multi-Camera Surveillance via Synthetic Domain Adaptation
item.dc:creator: Peggy Joy Lu
Wei-Yu Chen
Yao-Tsung Huang
Vincent Shin-Mu Tseng
item.description: Extensive experiments on multiple cross-domain detection benchmarks demonstrate that our method outperforms existing multi-source domain adaptation and federated learning baselines under multi-class, privacy-preserving settings. Our method improves mAP by +2.1% over prior privacy-preserving approaches and achieves a new state-of-the-art mAP of 33.4%, highlighting the effectiveness of HeroCrystal in enabling practical multi-camera AI surveillance systems. Published: 2026-04-25T15:10:51+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.804 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Peggy Joy Lu; Wei-Yu Chen; Yao-Tsung Huang; Vincent Shin-Mu Tseng

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104413

Relevance: 0.804 (must_read)

Extensive experiments on multiple cross-domain detection benchmarks demonstrate that our method outperforms existing multi-source domain adaptation and federated learning baselines under multi-class, privacy-preserving settings. Our method improves mAP by +2.1% over prior privacy-preserving approaches and achieves a new state-of-the-art mAP of 33.4%, highlighting the effectiveness of HeroCrystal in enabling practical multi-camera AI surveillance systems.

item.title: Unlocking Optical Prior: Spectrum-Guided Knowledge Transfer for SAR Generalized Category Discovery
item.dc:creator: Jingyuan Xia
Ruikang Hu
Ye Li
Zhixiong Yang
Xu Lan
Zhejun Lu
item.description: Generalized Category Discovery (GCD) holds significant promise for the label-scarce Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) domain, yet its efficacy is severely constrained by the cross-modal incompatibility between the inherent optical prior of the Large Vision Models (LVMs) and SAR imagery. Existing domain adaptation methods often lack an inductive bias that reflects imaging characteristics, consequently failing to effectively transfer optical prior into the SAR domain. To address this issue, the Modal Discrepancy Curve (MDC) is introduced to model cross-modal discrepancy as a structured frequency-domain descriptor derived from spectral energy distributions. Leveraging this formulation, we propose the MDC-guided Cross-modal Prior Transfer (MCPT) framework, a pre-training paradigm that operates on paired optical-SAR data. Within this framework, Adaptive Frequency Tokenization (AFT) converts the MDC into learnable tokens, and Frequency-aware Expert Refinement (FER) performs band-wise discrepancy-aware feature refinement using these tokens. Based on the refined representations, contrastive learning aligns refined embeddings across modalities and internalizes the adaptation pattern. Ultimately, the superior SAR feature representation capability learned during paired pre-training is applied to downstream single-modal SAR-GCD tasks. Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance across multiple mainstream datasets, indicating that frequency-domain discrepancy modeling enables more effective adaptation of optical prior to SAR imagery. Published: 2026-04-24T02:54:18+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.802 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jingyuan Xia; Ruikang Hu; Ye Li; Zhixiong Yang; Xu Lan; Zhejun Lu

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.802 (must_read)

Generalized Category Discovery (GCD) holds significant promise for the label-scarce Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) domain, yet its efficacy is severely constrained by the cross-modal incompatibility between the inherent optical prior of the Large Vision Models (LVMs) and SAR imagery. Existing domain adaptation methods often lack an inductive bias that reflects imaging characteristics, consequently failing to effectively transfer optical prior into the SAR domain. To address this issue, the Modal Discrepancy Curve (MDC) is introduced to model cross-modal discrepancy as a structured frequency-domain descriptor derived from spectral energy distributions. Leveraging this formulation, we propose the MDC-guided Cross-modal Prior Transfer (MCPT) framework, a pre-training paradigm that operates on paired optical-SAR data. Within this framework, Adaptive Frequency Tokenization (AFT) converts the MDC into learnable tokens, and Frequency-aware Expert Refinement (FER) performs band-wise discrepancy-aware feature refinement using these tokens. Based on the refined representations, contrastive learning aligns refined embeddings across modalities and internalizes the adaptation pattern. Ultimately, the superior SAR feature representation capability learned during paired pre-training is applied to downstream single-modal SAR-GCD tasks. Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance across multiple mainstream datasets, indicating that frequency-domain discrepancy modeling enables more effective adaptation of optical prior to SAR imagery.

item.title: Real2Real: Invariant Style Alignment for Unsupervised One-Shot Real Noisy Image Denoising
item.dc:creator: Ruijun Ma
Fubin Pan
Pingwen Liang
Liudan Diao
Jiteng Li
Bob Zhang
Long Qi
item.description: Recent advances in unsupervised learning have substantially boosted real image denoising, enabling improved usability and generalization. In this paper, we tackle a realistic yet more challenging task, termed one-shot unsupervised real image denoising, which strives to recover a single unlabeled real image without prior noise knowledge. To address this setting, we propose a framework that leverages Real2Real one-shot style-aware adaptation, adapting a source-pretrained denoiser to generalize to the target image. Specifically, we introduce a novel Style Alignment Encoding (SAE) module complemented with a Style Gating Modulation (SGM) operator. SGM learns dynamic thresholds to enhance style alignment, enabling SAE to generate features well-aligned with the target style. In tandem, we devise an Adaptive Style Mining (ASM) scheme that perturbs target style representations via gradient updates. This process uncovers complex yet plausible style variations beyond the one-shot target image, synthesizing new stylized instances that drive the denoiser to adapt to a broader range of styles. By combining the SAE model and the denoiser with the ASM scheme, our framework iteratively synthesizes new stylized images and adapts the denoiser, significantly enhancing generalization and robustness. Extensive experiments on real-world benchmarks demonstrate that our approach achieves superior performance versus state-of-the-art unsupervised methods, demonstrating enhanced generalization and strong potential for practical denoising. Published: 2026-04-25T15:45:09+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.801 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ruijun Ma; Fubin Pan; Pingwen Liang; Liudan Diao; Jiteng Li; Bob Zhang; Long Qi

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104410

Relevance: 0.801 (must_read)

Recent advances in unsupervised learning have substantially boosted real image denoising, enabling improved usability and generalization. In this paper, we tackle a realistic yet more challenging task, termed one-shot unsupervised real image denoising, which strives to recover a single unlabeled real image without prior noise knowledge. To address this setting, we propose a framework that leverages Real2Real one-shot style-aware adaptation, adapting a source-pretrained denoiser to generalize to the target image. Specifically, we introduce a novel Style Alignment Encoding (SAE) module complemented with a Style Gating Modulation (SGM) operator. SGM learns dynamic thresholds to enhance style alignment, enabling SAE to generate features well-aligned with the target style. In tandem, we devise an Adaptive Style Mining (ASM) scheme that perturbs target style representations via gradient updates. This process uncovers complex yet plausible style variations beyond the one-shot target image, synthesizing new stylized instances that drive the denoiser to adapt to a broader range of styles. By combining the SAE model and the denoiser with the ASM scheme, our framework iteratively synthesizes new stylized images and adapts the denoiser, significantly enhancing generalization and robustness. Extensive experiments on real-world benchmarks demonstrate that our approach achieves superior performance versus state-of-the-art unsupervised methods, demonstrating enhanced generalization and strong potential for practical denoising.

item.title: UHR-DETR: Efficient End-to-End Small Object Detection for Ultra-High-Resolution Remote Sensing Imagery
item.dc:creator: Jingfang Li
Haoran Zhu
Wen Yang
Jinrui Zhang
Fang Xu
Haijian Zhang
Gui-Song Xia
item.description: Ultra-High-Resolution (UHR) imagery has become essential for modern remote sensing, offering unprecedented spatial coverage. However, detecting small objects in such vast scenes presents a critical dilemma: retaining the original resolution for small objects causes prohibitive memory bottlenecks. Conversely, conventional compromises like image downsampling or patch cropping either erase small objects or destroy context. To break this dilemma, we propose UHR-DETR, an efficient end-to-end transformer-based detector designed for UHR imagery. First, we introduce a Coverage-Maximizing Sparse Encoder that dynamically allocates finite computational resources to informative high-resolution regions, ensuring maximum object coverage with minimal spatial redundancy. Second, we design a Global-Local Decoupled Decoder. By integrating macroscopic scene awareness with microscopic object details, this module resolves semantic ambiguities and prevents scene fragmentation. Extensive experiments on the UHR imagery datasets (e.g., STAR and SODA-A) demonstrate the superiority of UHR-DETR under strict hardware constraints (e.g., a single 24GB RTX 3090). It achieves a 2.8\% mAP improvement while delivering a 10$\times$ inference speedup compared to standard sliding-window baselines on the STAR dataset. Our codes and models will be available at https://github.com/Li-JingFang/UHR-DETR. Published: 2026-04-23T08:51:24+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.792 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jingfang Li; Haoran Zhu; Wen Yang; Jinrui Zhang; Fang Xu; Haijian Zhang; Gui-Song Xia

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.792 (must_read)

Ultra-High-Resolution (UHR) imagery has become essential for modern remote sensing, offering unprecedented spatial coverage. However, detecting small objects in such vast scenes presents a critical dilemma: retaining the original resolution for small objects causes prohibitive memory bottlenecks. Conversely, conventional compromises like image downsampling or patch cropping either erase small objects or destroy context. To break this dilemma, we propose UHR-DETR, an efficient end-to-end transformer-based detector designed for UHR imagery. First, we introduce a Coverage-Maximizing Sparse Encoder that dynamically allocates finite computational resources to informative high-resolution regions, ensuring maximum object coverage with minimal spatial redundancy. Second, we design a Global-Local Decoupled Decoder. By integrating macroscopic scene awareness with microscopic object details, this module resolves semantic ambiguities and prevents scene fragmentation. Extensive experiments on the UHR imagery datasets (e.g., STAR and SODA-A) demonstrate the superiority of UHR-DETR under strict hardware constraints (e.g., a single 24GB RTX 3090). It achieves a 2.8\% mAP improvement while delivering a 10$\times$ inference speedup compared to standard sliding-window baselines on the STAR dataset. Our codes and models will be available at https://github.com/Li-JingFang/UHR-DETR.

item.title: Mutually Causal Semantic Distillation Network for Zero-Shot Learning
item.dc:creator: Shiming Chen
Shuhuang Chen
Guo-Sen Xie
Xinge You
item.description: Zero-shot learning (ZSL) aims to recognize the unseen classes in the open-world guided by the side-information (e.g., attributes). Its key task is how to infer the latent semantic knowledge between visual and attribute features on seen classes, and thus conducting a desirable semantic knowledge transfer from seen classes to unseen ones. Prior works simply utilize unidirectional attention within a weakly-supervised manner to learn the spurious and limited latent semantic representations, which fail to effectively discover the intrinsic semantic knowledge (e.g., attribute semantic) between visual and attribute features. To solve the above challenges, we propose a mutually causal semantic distillation network (termed MSDN++) to distill the intrinsic and sufficient semantic representations for ZSL. MSDN++ consists of an attribute $$ightarrow $$ visual causal attention sub-net that learns attribute-based visual features, and a visual $$ightarrow $$ attribute causal attention sub-net that learns visual-based attribute features. The causal attentions encourages the two sub-nets to learn causal vision-attribute associations for representing reliable features with causal visual/attribute learning. With the guidance of semantic distillation loss, the two mutual attention sub-nets learn collaboratively and teach each other throughout the training process. Extensive experiments on three widely-used benchmark datasets (e.g., CUB, SUN, AWA2, and FLO) show that our MSDN++ yields significant improvements over the strong baselines, leading to new state-of-the-art performances. Published: 2026-04-25T19:22:58+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Computer Vision Score: 0.792 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Shiming Chen; Shuhuang Chen; Guo-Sen Xie; Xinge You

Journal: International Journal of Computer Vision

DOI: 10.1007/s11263-026-02814-2

Relevance: 0.792 (must_read)

Zero-shot learning (ZSL) aims to recognize the unseen classes in the open-world guided by the side-information (e.g., attributes). Its key task is how to infer the latent semantic knowledge between visual and attribute features on seen classes, and thus conducting a desirable semantic knowledge transfer from seen classes to unseen ones. Prior works simply utilize unidirectional attention within a weakly-supervised manner to learn the spurious and limited latent semantic representations, which fail to effectively discover the intrinsic semantic knowledge (e.g., attribute semantic) between visual and attribute features. To solve the above challenges, we propose a mutually causal semantic distillation network (termed MSDN++) to distill the intrinsic and sufficient semantic representations for ZSL. MSDN++ consists of an attribute $$ightarrow $$ visual causal attention sub-net that learns attribute-based visual features, and a visual $$ightarrow $$ attribute causal attention sub-net that learns visual-based attribute features. The causal attentions encourages the two sub-nets to learn causal vision-attribute associations for representing reliable features with causal visual/attribute learning. With the guidance of semantic distillation loss, the two mutual attention sub-nets learn collaboratively and teach each other throughout the training process. Extensive experiments on three widely-used benchmark datasets (e.g., CUB, SUN, AWA2, and FLO) show that our MSDN++ yields significant improvements over the strong baselines, leading to new state-of-the-art performances.

item.title: Bridging semantics and geometry: A decoupled LVLM–SAM framework for reasoning segmentation in optical remote sensing
item.dc:creator: Xu Zhang
Junyao Ge
Yang Zheng
Kaitai Guo
Jimin Liang
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Large Vision–Language Models (LVLMs) hold great promise for advancing optical remote sensing (RS) analysis, yet existing reasoning segmentation frameworks couple linguistic reasoning and pixel prediction through end-to-end supervised fine-tuning, leading to weak geometric grounding and limited generalization across tasks. To address this, we developed Think2Seg-RS, a decoupled framework that trains an LVLM prompter to control a frozen Segment Anything Model (SAM) via structured geometric prompts. Through a mask-only Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) reinforcement learning objective driven strictly by final mask IoU, the LVLM learns to translate abstract semantic reasoning into spatially grounded actions, achieving state-of-the-art performance on the EarthReason dataset. Notably, Think2Seg-RS outperforms leading approaches such as RemoteReasoner and SegEarth-R1 on the EarthReason dataset by reaching a test cIoU of 75.60% and gIoU of 73.36%, yielding absolute improvements of 6.47% and 2.40% over the strongest baseline, respectively. Zero-shot evaluations across three referring segmentation benchmarks reveal a fundamental distinction in task inductive bias, exposing a distinct divide between semantic-level grounding—which aggregates all regions matching a conceptual intent—and instance-level tasks that demand discrete object separation. We further found that compact segmenters outperform larger ones under semantic-level supervision by mitigating textural over-segmentation, and that unconstrained negative prompting is unstable in heterogeneous aerial backgrounds. Together, these findings demonstrate that optimizing LVLMs through direct segmentation feedback offers a scalable framework for complex geospatial reasoning, effectively bridging the gap between abstract language understanding and precise pixel-level execution. Our code and model are available at https://github.com/Ricardo-XZ/Think2Seg-RS . Published: 2026-04-25T10:52:38+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.789 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xu Zhang; Junyao Ge; Yang Zheng; Kaitai Guo; Jimin Liang

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.04.036

Relevance: 0.789 (must_read)

Large Vision–Language Models (LVLMs) hold great promise for advancing optical remote sensing (RS) analysis, yet existing reasoning segmentation frameworks couple linguistic reasoning and pixel prediction through end-to-end supervised fine-tuning, leading to weak geometric grounding and limited generalization across tasks. To address this, we developed Think2Seg-RS, a decoupled framework that trains an LVLM prompter to control a frozen Segment Anything Model (SAM) via structured geometric prompts. Through a mask-only Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) reinforcement learning objective driven strictly by final mask IoU, the LVLM learns to translate abstract semantic reasoning into spatially grounded actions, achieving state-of-the-art performance on the EarthReason dataset. Notably, Think2Seg-RS outperforms leading approaches such as RemoteReasoner and SegEarth-R1 on the EarthReason dataset by reaching a test cIoU of 75.60% and gIoU of 73.36%, yielding absolute improvements of 6.47% and 2.40% over the strongest baseline, respectively. Zero-shot evaluations across three referring segmentation benchmarks reveal a fundamental distinction in task inductive bias, exposing a distinct divide between semantic-level grounding—which aggregates all regions matching a conceptual intent—and instance-level tasks that demand discrete object separation. We further found that compact segmenters outperform larger ones under semantic-level supervision by mitigating textural over-segmentation, and that unconstrained negative prompting is unstable in heterogeneous aerial backgrounds. Together, these findings demonstrate that optimizing LVLMs through direct segmentation feedback offers a scalable framework for complex geospatial reasoning, effectively bridging the gap between abstract language understanding and precise pixel-level execution. Our code and model are available at https://github.com/Ricardo-XZ/Think2Seg-RS .

item.title: Distilling Vision Transformers for Distortion-Robust Representation Learning
item.dc:creator: Konstantinos Alexis
Giorgos Giannopoulos
Dimitrios Gunopulos
item.description: Self-supervised learning has achieved remarkable success in learning visual representations from clean data, yet remains challenging when clean observations are sparse or not available at all. In this paper, we demonstrate that pretrained vision models can be leveraged to learn distortion-robust representations, which can then be effectively applied to downstream tasks operating on distorted observations. In particular, we propose an asymmetric knowledge distillation framework in which both teacher and student are initialized from the same pretrained Vision Transformer but receive different views of each image: the teacher processes clean images, while the student sees their distorted versions. We introduce multi-level distillation that aligns global embeddings, patch-level features, and attention maps and show that the student is able to approximate clean-image representations despite never directly accessing clean data. We evaluate our approach on image classification tasks across several datasets and under various distortions, consistently outperforming existing alternatives for the same amount of human supervision. Published: 2026-04-24T13:15:26+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.783 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Konstantinos Alexis; Giorgos Giannopoulos; Dimitrios Gunopulos

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.783 (must_read)

Self-supervised learning has achieved remarkable success in learning visual representations from clean data, yet remains challenging when clean observations are sparse or not available at all. In this paper, we demonstrate that pretrained vision models can be leveraged to learn distortion-robust representations, which can then be effectively applied to downstream tasks operating on distorted observations. In particular, we propose an asymmetric knowledge distillation framework in which both teacher and student are initialized from the same pretrained Vision Transformer but receive different views of each image: the teacher processes clean images, while the student sees their distorted versions. We introduce multi-level distillation that aligns global embeddings, patch-level features, and attention maps and show that the student is able to approximate clean-image representations despite never directly accessing clean data. We evaluate our approach on image classification tasks across several datasets and under various distortions, consistently outperforming existing alternatives for the same amount of human supervision.

item.title: Mult-Pool Self Attention: a lightweight attention with linear complexity
item.dc:creator: Seyed Mohammad Hatefi
Hossein Mahvash Mohammadi
Hamidreza Baradaran Kashani
item.description: Self-attention has been widely adopted in computer vision due to its ability to capture global context. However, its quadratic time complexity limits its applicability in real-time scenarios, particularly on resource-constrained devices. To address this limitation, hybrid architectures combine attention mechanisms with conventional convolutions to balance accuracy and efficiency. These models typically apply attention only in the later stages of the network, where the spatial resolution is reduced, to mitigate computational cost. A key limitation of standard self-attention lies in its reliance on computationally expensive matrix operations. In this paper, we propose a linear attention mechanism that eliminates matrix multiplication and performs attention using lightweight element-wise multiplication and a simple pooling operation. We term this mechanism Mult-Pool Self Attention (MPSA), and the resulting architecture is referred to as MultPoolFormer. Experimental results on ImageNet-1K demonstrate that MultPoolFormer achieves a strong balance between accuracy and efficiency. Our XS model, with 2.9M parameters, attains 76.9% top-1 accuracy while maintaining a compact design. The S model achieves 79.8% accuracy with only 5M parameters and 0.9 GFLOPs. The M and L variants, with 11M and 19M parameters, respectively, reach 82.2% and 83.3% top-1 accuracy. The proposed architecture also demonstrates strong generalization across downstream tasks, including object detection, instance segmentation, and semantic segmentation. Across all model scales, MultPoolFormer achieves a favorable trade-off between accuracy and computational efficiency, making it well-suited for resource-constrained vision applications. Published: 2026-04-25T23:57:08+00:00 Venue: Knowledge-Based Systems Score: 0.783 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Seyed Mohammad Hatefi; Hossein Mahvash Mohammadi; Hamidreza Baradaran Kashani

Journal: Knowledge-Based Systems

DOI: 10.1016/j.knosys.2026.116082

Relevance: 0.783 (must_read)

Self-attention has been widely adopted in computer vision due to its ability to capture global context. However, its quadratic time complexity limits its applicability in real-time scenarios, particularly on resource-constrained devices. To address this limitation, hybrid architectures combine attention mechanisms with conventional convolutions to balance accuracy and efficiency. These models typically apply attention only in the later stages of the network, where the spatial resolution is reduced, to mitigate computational cost. A key limitation of standard self-attention lies in its reliance on computationally expensive matrix operations. In this paper, we propose a linear attention mechanism that eliminates matrix multiplication and performs attention using lightweight element-wise multiplication and a simple pooling operation. We term this mechanism Mult-Pool Self Attention (MPSA), and the resulting architecture is referred to as MultPoolFormer. Experimental results on ImageNet-1K demonstrate that MultPoolFormer achieves a strong balance between accuracy and efficiency. Our XS model, with 2.9M parameters, attains 76.9% top-1 accuracy while maintaining a compact design. The S model achieves 79.8% accuracy with only 5M parameters and 0.9 GFLOPs. The M and L variants, with 11M and 19M parameters, respectively, reach 82.2% and 83.3% top-1 accuracy. The proposed architecture also demonstrates strong generalization across downstream tasks, including object detection, instance segmentation, and semantic segmentation. Across all model scales, MultPoolFormer achieves a favorable trade-off between accuracy and computational efficiency, making it well-suited for resource-constrained vision applications.

item.title: Physically-aware deep learning for reconstructing gap-free sea surface temperature in the South China Sea
item.dc:creator: Changwei Su
Siyu Chen
Jun Zhao
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Missing data caused by cloud cover, sensor limitations, and orbital gaps remain a fundamental challenge for many satellite-derived geophysical products, limiting their utility in environmental monitoring and climate research. To address this issue, we propose a physically informed deep learning framework, the Peripheral Feature-assisted Fourier-transform Convolutional Bidirectional LSTM (PFTCBiLSTM), for reconstructing spatiotemporally coherent fields from incomplete remote sensing imagery. The model combines Fourier-domain convolutional layers, which capture spatial frequency characteristics, with bidirectional recurrent units that represent nonlinear temporal dependencies, while auxiliary geophysical drivers provide physical context for air–sea interactions. Using geostationary satellite observations over the South China Sea as a test case, PFTCBiLSTM demonstrated substantial improvements over advanced baseline models. The final configuration reduced the mean absolute error (MAE) from 0.33 °C for FTC-LSTM to 0.21 °C and the root mean square error (RMSE) from 0.51 °C to 0.35 °C, while increasing the structural similarity index (SSIM) to 0.98 and maintaining R 2 = 0.98. Independent validation against NOAA iQuam in situ observations confirmed robust performance across both deep-basin and coastal regimes, particularly in cloud-prone and dynamically complex regions. Sensitivity experiments, Shapley Additive Explanations (SHAP), and adversarial robustness tests using projected gradient descent (PGD) further highlighted the model’s stability and interpretability. Although demonstrated here for an oceanographic application, the proposed framework offers a transferable solution for remote sensing image reconstruction. Its design can be readily extended to other satellite-derived variables, including chlorophyll-a concentration, aerosol optical depth, and salinity, providing a versatile methodology for advancing spatiotemporal data recovery across diverse Earth observation domains. Published: 2026-04-26T19:19:57+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.782 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Changwei Su; Siyu Chen; Jun Zhao

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.04.016

Relevance: 0.782 (must_read)

Missing data caused by cloud cover, sensor limitations, and orbital gaps remain a fundamental challenge for many satellite-derived geophysical products, limiting their utility in environmental monitoring and climate research. To address this issue, we propose a physically informed deep learning framework, the Peripheral Feature-assisted Fourier-transform Convolutional Bidirectional LSTM (PFTCBiLSTM), for reconstructing spatiotemporally coherent fields from incomplete remote sensing imagery. The model combines Fourier-domain convolutional layers, which capture spatial frequency characteristics, with bidirectional recurrent units that represent nonlinear temporal dependencies, while auxiliary geophysical drivers provide physical context for air–sea interactions. Using geostationary satellite observations over the South China Sea as a test case, PFTCBiLSTM demonstrated substantial improvements over advanced baseline models. The final configuration reduced the mean absolute error (MAE) from 0.33 °C for FTC-LSTM to 0.21 °C and the root mean square error (RMSE) from 0.51 °C to 0.35 °C, while increasing the structural similarity index (SSIM) to 0.98 and maintaining R 2 = 0.98. Independent validation against NOAA iQuam in situ observations confirmed robust performance across both deep-basin and coastal regimes, particularly in cloud-prone and dynamically complex regions. Sensitivity experiments, Shapley Additive Explanations (SHAP), and adversarial robustness tests using projected gradient descent (PGD) further highlighted the model’s stability and interpretability. Although demonstrated here for an oceanographic application, the proposed framework offers a transferable solution for remote sensing image reconstruction. Its design can be readily extended to other satellite-derived variables, including chlorophyll-a concentration, aerosol optical depth, and salinity, providing a versatile methodology for advancing spatiotemporal data recovery across diverse Earth observation domains.

item.title: UFFL: Bridging Slide- and Cell-Level Annotations via Unified Feature Fusion Learning for Cervical Cancer Screening
item.dc:creator: Manman Fei
Zhiyun Song
Zhenrong Shen
Mengjun Liu
Qian Wang
Lichi Zhang
item.description: Cervical cancer screening involves the classification of whole slide images (WSI) and the detection of cervical lesion cells. Deep learning for cervical lesion analysis typically demands extensive cell-level annotations, which are costly and time-consuming to obtain. Conversely, weak slide-level annotations, assigning a single label to a gigapixel WSI, are more accessible but lack detailed lesion cell information. In this paper, we propose the Unified Feature Fusion Learning (UFFL) framework to optimize both WSI classification and cell detection in cervical cancer screening. By bridging slide- and cell-level annotations, UFFL employs weakly supervised learning via the CerviBank Refinement Unit (CBRU), which leverages weak slide-level annotations (WSA) to integrate limited cell-level annotations (LCA). It leverages context-enhanced Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) to address the annotation scale gap between tasks. The CBRU captures distinct positive and negative cell patterns to enhance feature representation within MIL. Additionally, Noise-Suppressed Positive Guidance (NSPG) improves latent space robustness by prioritizing positive instances, reducing noise, and enhancing separability from ambiguous regions. Experimental results demonstrate that UFFL significantly improves detection and classification performance across diverse annotation levels. Published: 2026-04-26T14:46:27+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.779 (must_read)

Authors: Manman Fei; Zhiyun Song; Zhenrong Shen; Mengjun Liu; Qian Wang; Lichi Zhang

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104427

Relevance: 0.779 (must_read)

Cervical cancer screening involves the classification of whole slide images (WSI) and the detection of cervical lesion cells. Deep learning for cervical lesion analysis typically demands extensive cell-level annotations, which are costly and time-consuming to obtain. Conversely, weak slide-level annotations, assigning a single label to a gigapixel WSI, are more accessible but lack detailed lesion cell information. In this paper, we propose the Unified Feature Fusion Learning (UFFL) framework to optimize both WSI classification and cell detection in cervical cancer screening. By bridging slide- and cell-level annotations, UFFL employs weakly supervised learning via the CerviBank Refinement Unit (CBRU), which leverages weak slide-level annotations (WSA) to integrate limited cell-level annotations (LCA). It leverages context-enhanced Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) to address the annotation scale gap between tasks. The CBRU captures distinct positive and negative cell patterns to enhance feature representation within MIL. Additionally, Noise-Suppressed Positive Guidance (NSPG) improves latent space robustness by prioritizing positive instances, reducing noise, and enhancing separability from ambiguous regions. Experimental results demonstrate that UFFL significantly improves detection and classification performance across diverse annotation levels.

item.title: VFM$^{4}$SDG: Unveiling the Power of VFMs for Single-Domain Generalized Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Yupeng Zhang
Ruize Han
Ningnan Guo
Wei Feng
Song Wang
Liang Wan
item.description: In real-world scenarios, continual changes in weather, illumination, and imaging conditions cause significant domain shifts, leading detectors trained on a single source domain to degrade severely in unseen environments. Existing single-domain generalized object detection (SDGOD) methods mainly rely on data augmentation or domain-invariant representation learning, but pay limited attention to detector mechanisms, leaving clear limitations under complex domain shifts. Through analytical experiments, we find that performance degradation is dominated by increasing missed detections, which fundamentally arises from reduced cross-domain stability of the detector: object-background and inter-instance relations become less stable in the encoding stage, while semantic-spatial alignment of query representations also becomes harder to maintain in the decoding stage. To this end, we propose VFM$^{4}$SDG, a dual-prior learning framework for SDGOD, which introduces a frozen vision foundation model (VFM) as a transferable cross-domain stability prior into detector representation learning and query modeling. In the encoding stage, we propose Cross-domain Stable Relational Prior Distillation to enhance the robustness of object-background and inter-instance relational modeling. In the decoding stage, we propose Semantic-Contextual Prior-based Query Enhancement, which injects category-level semantic prototypes and global visual context into queries to improve their semantic recognition and spatial localization stability in unseen domains. Extensive experiments show that the proposed method consistently outperforms existing SOTA methods on standard SDGOD benchmarks and two mainstream DETR-based detectors, demonstrating its effectiveness, robustness, and generality. Published: 2026-04-23T10:04:36+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.779 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yupeng Zhang; Ruize Han; Ningnan Guo; Wei Feng; Song Wang; Liang Wan

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.779 (must_read)

In real-world scenarios, continual changes in weather, illumination, and imaging conditions cause significant domain shifts, leading detectors trained on a single source domain to degrade severely in unseen environments. Existing single-domain generalized object detection (SDGOD) methods mainly rely on data augmentation or domain-invariant representation learning, but pay limited attention to detector mechanisms, leaving clear limitations under complex domain shifts. Through analytical experiments, we find that performance degradation is dominated by increasing missed detections, which fundamentally arises from reduced cross-domain stability of the detector: object-background and inter-instance relations become less stable in the encoding stage, while semantic-spatial alignment of query representations also becomes harder to maintain in the decoding stage. To this end, we propose VFM$^{4}$SDG, a dual-prior learning framework for SDGOD, which introduces a frozen vision foundation model (VFM) as a transferable cross-domain stability prior into detector representation learning and query modeling. In the encoding stage, we propose Cross-domain Stable Relational Prior Distillation to enhance the robustness of object-background and inter-instance relational modeling. In the decoding stage, we propose Semantic-Contextual Prior-based Query Enhancement, which injects category-level semantic prototypes and global visual context into queries to improve their semantic recognition and spatial localization stability in unseen domains. Extensive experiments show that the proposed method consistently outperforms existing SOTA methods on standard SDGOD benchmarks and two mainstream DETR-based detectors, demonstrating its effectiveness, robustness, and generality.

item.title: Improving the generalization of ViTs for action understanding with VLM pre-training
item.dc:creator: Hui Lu
Albert Ali Salah
Ronald Poppe
item.description: Owing to their ability to extract powerful video embeddings, Vision Transformers (ViTs) are currently the best performing models in video action understanding. However, when these models are frozen and applied to downstream tasks, their performance drops significantly, revealing limited generalization. In this paper, we describe the Four-Tiered Prompts (FTP) framework that introduces feature processors to transform the ViT’s output. In a pre-training stage, each feature processor is trained using contrastive learning to align the ViT’s visual embeddings with a vision language model’s (VLM) textual embeddings. We use four feature processors, each linked to the output of a VLM prompt that reflects the fundamental aspects of human action: category, components, description, and context. With the FTP framework, we increase the ViT’s generalization ability by forcing the visual encoder to incorporate relevant, semantic information. Importantly, we only employ the VLM during training. Subsequently, inference incurs a limited computation cost. For video action recognition and detection, employing the FTP framework consistently yields state-of-the-art performance after fine-tuning. Extensive experiments demonstrate how different choices contribute to the overall increase in performance. 1 Published: 2026-04-25T15:03:59+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.778 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Hui Lu; Albert Ali Salah; Ronald Poppe

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.113794

Relevance: 0.778 (must_read)

Owing to their ability to extract powerful video embeddings, Vision Transformers (ViTs) are currently the best performing models in video action understanding. However, when these models are frozen and applied to downstream tasks, their performance drops significantly, revealing limited generalization. In this paper, we describe the Four-Tiered Prompts (FTP) framework that introduces feature processors to transform the ViT’s output. In a pre-training stage, each feature processor is trained using contrastive learning to align the ViT’s visual embeddings with a vision language model’s (VLM) textual embeddings. We use four feature processors, each linked to the output of a VLM prompt that reflects the fundamental aspects of human action: category, components, description, and context. With the FTP framework, we increase the ViT’s generalization ability by forcing the visual encoder to incorporate relevant, semantic information. Importantly, we only employ the VLM during training. Subsequently, inference incurs a limited computation cost. For video action recognition and detection, employing the FTP framework consistently yields state-of-the-art performance after fine-tuning. Extensive experiments demonstrate how different choices contribute to the overall increase in performance. 1

item.title: ICPR 2026 Competition on Low-Resolution License Plate Recognition
item.dc:creator: Rayson Laroca
Valfride Nascimento
Donggun Kim
Sanghyeok Chung
Subin Bae
Uihwan Seo
Seungsang Oh
Chi M. Phung
Minh G. Vo
Xingsong Ye
Yongkun Du
Yuchen Su
Zhineng Chen
Sunhee Heo
Hyangwoo Lee
Kihyun Na
Khanh V. Vu Nguyen
Sang T. Pham
Duc N. N. Phung
Trong P. Le
Vy N. Vo Tran
David Menotti
item.description: Low-Resolution License Plate Recognition (LRLPR) remains a challenging problem in real-world surveillance scenarios, where long capture distances, compression artifacts, and adverse imaging conditions can severely degrade license plate legibility. To promote progress in this area, we organized the ICPR 2026 Competition on Low-Resolution License Plate Recognition, the first competition specifically dedicated to LRLPR using real low-quality data collected under operationally relevant conditions. The competition was based on the LRLPR-26 dataset, which comprises 20,000 training tracks and 3,000 test tracks; each training track contains five low-resolution and five high-resolution images of the same license plate. Notably, a total of 269 teams from 41 countries registered for the competition, and 99 teams submitted valid entries in the Blind Test Phase. The winning team achieved a Recognition Rate of 82.13%, and four teams surpassed the 80% mark, highlighting both the high level of competition at the top of the leaderboard and the continued difficulty of the task. In addition to presenting the competition design, evaluation protocol, and main results, this paper summarizes the methods adopted by the top-5 teams and discusses current trends and promising directions for future research on LRLPR. The competition webpage is available at https://icpr26lrlpr.github.io/ Published: 2026-04-24T12:36:09+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.777 (must_read)

Vo; Xingsong Ye; Yongkun Du; Yuchen Su; Zhineng Chen; Sunhee Heo; Hyangwoo Lee; Kihyun Na; Khanh V. Vu Nguyen; Sang T. Pham; Duc N. N. Phung; Trong P. Le; Vy N. Vo Tran; David Menotti

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.777 (must_read)

Low-Resolution License Plate Recognition (LRLPR) remains a challenging problem in real-world surveillance scenarios, where long capture distances, compression artifacts, and adverse imaging conditions can severely degrade license plate legibility. To promote progress in this area, we organized the ICPR 2026 Competition on Low-Resolution License Plate Recognition, the first competition specifically dedicated to LRLPR using real low-quality data collected under operationally relevant conditions. The competition was based on the LRLPR-26 dataset, which comprises 20,000 training tracks and 3,000 test tracks; each training track contains five low-resolution and five high-resolution images of the same license plate. Notably, a total of 269 teams from 41 countries registered for the competition, and 99 teams submitted valid entries in the Blind Test Phase. The winning team achieved a Recognition Rate of 82.13%, and four teams surpassed the 80% mark, highlighting both the high level of competition at the top of the leaderboard and the continued difficulty of the task. In addition to presenting the competition design, evaluation protocol, and main results, this paper summarizes the methods adopted by the top-5 teams and discusses current trends and promising directions for future research on LRLPR. The competition webpage is available at https://icpr26lrlpr.github.io/

item.title: Query-guided graph contrastive prototype refined network for cross-domain hyperspectral image change detection
item.dc:creator: Wenhui Hou
Jiangtao Peng
Bing Yang
Lanxin Wu
Weiwei Sun
item.description: Due to the difficulties in obtaining labeled samples of hyperspectral images (HSI), few-shot learning (FSL) has been applied to hyperspectral change detection (CD) by virtue of its excellent learning ability. However, existing FSL-based methods fail to fully consider the structural correlation between samples and the instability of prototypes. To solve the above issues, this paper proposes a query-guided graph contrastive prototype refined network (QGCPRN) for cross-domain HSI CD, which effectively integrates graph contrastive learning, prototype correction and FSL into a unified framework, alleviating the domain transfer problem caused by differences in data type. Specifically, we designed a graph constraint contrastive module to enhance the compactness of samples within a class and the separability of samples between classes by leveraging the topological structural relationship between samples to strengthen the class boundaries. Meanwhile, in order to further optimize the discriminative power of the prototypes, a top-K query-guided prototype refined module is designed to dynamically correct the support prototypes. In addition, the proposed cross-domain bidirectional prototype-instance contrastive method is constructed to align the feature distribution between the source and target domains. Experiments on three hyperspectral CD datasets show that the proposed QGCPRN algorithm outperforms existing deep learning or small sample learning algorithms. Published: 2026-04-25T15:08:35+00:00 Venue: Knowledge-Based Systems Score: 0.776 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Wenhui Hou; Jiangtao Peng; Bing Yang; Lanxin Wu; Weiwei Sun

Journal: Knowledge-Based Systems

DOI: 10.1016/j.knosys.2026.116042

Relevance: 0.776 (must_read)

Due to the difficulties in obtaining labeled samples of hyperspectral images (HSI), few-shot learning (FSL) has been applied to hyperspectral change detection (CD) by virtue of its excellent learning ability. However, existing FSL-based methods fail to fully consider the structural correlation between samples and the instability of prototypes. To solve the above issues, this paper proposes a query-guided graph contrastive prototype refined network (QGCPRN) for cross-domain HSI CD, which effectively integrates graph contrastive learning, prototype correction and FSL into a unified framework, alleviating the domain transfer problem caused by differences in data type. Specifically, we designed a graph constraint contrastive module to enhance the compactness of samples within a class and the separability of samples between classes by leveraging the topological structural relationship between samples to strengthen the class boundaries. Meanwhile, in order to further optimize the discriminative power of the prototypes, a top-K query-guided prototype refined module is designed to dynamically correct the support prototypes. In addition, the proposed cross-domain bidirectional prototype-instance contrastive method is constructed to align the feature distribution between the source and target domains. Experiments on three hyperspectral CD datasets show that the proposed QGCPRN algorithm outperforms existing deep learning or small sample learning algorithms.

item.title: SyMTRS: Benchmark Multi-Task Synthetic Dataset for Depth, Domain Adaptation and Super-Resolution in Aerial Imagery
item.dc:creator: Safouane El Ghazouali
Nicola Venturi
Michael Rueegsegger
Umberto Michelucci
item.description: Recent advances in deep learning for remote sensing rely heavily on large annotated datasets, yet acquiring high-quality ground truth for geometric, radiometric, and multi-domain tasks remains costly and often infeasible. In particular, the lack of accurate depth annotations, controlled illumination variations, and multi-scale paired imagery limits progress in monocular depth estimation, domain adaptation, and super-resolution for aerial scenes. We present SyMTRS, a large-scale synthetic dataset generated using a high-fidelity urban simulation pipeline. The dataset provides high-resolution RGB aerial imagery (2048 x 2048), pixel-perfect depth maps, night-time counterparts for domain adaptation, and aligned low-resolution variants for super-resolution at x2, x4, and x8 scales. Unlike existing remote sensing datasets that focus on a single task or modality, SyMTRS is designed as a unified multi-task benchmark enabling joint research in geometric understanding, cross-domain robustness, and resolution enhancement. We describe the dataset generation process, its statistical properties, and its positioning relative to existing benchmarks. SyMTRS aims to bridge critical gaps in remote sensing research by enabling controlled experiments with perfect geometric ground truth and consistent multi-domain supervision. The results obtained in this work can be reproduced from this Github repository: https://github.com/safouaneelg/SyMTRS. Published: 2026-04-23T15:59:12+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.773 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Safouane El Ghazouali; Nicola Venturi; Michael Rueegsegger; Umberto Michelucci

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.773 (must_read)

Recent advances in deep learning for remote sensing rely heavily on large annotated datasets, yet acquiring high-quality ground truth for geometric, radiometric, and multi-domain tasks remains costly and often infeasible. In particular, the lack of accurate depth annotations, controlled illumination variations, and multi-scale paired imagery limits progress in monocular depth estimation, domain adaptation, and super-resolution for aerial scenes. We present SyMTRS, a large-scale synthetic dataset generated using a high-fidelity urban simulation pipeline. The dataset provides high-resolution RGB aerial imagery (2048 x 2048), pixel-perfect depth maps, night-time counterparts for domain adaptation, and aligned low-resolution variants for super-resolution at x2, x4, and x8 scales. Unlike existing remote sensing datasets that focus on a single task or modality, SyMTRS is designed as a unified multi-task benchmark enabling joint research in geometric understanding, cross-domain robustness, and resolution enhancement. We describe the dataset generation process, its statistical properties, and its positioning relative to existing benchmarks. SyMTRS aims to bridge critical gaps in remote sensing research by enabling controlled experiments with perfect geometric ground truth and consistent multi-domain supervision. The results obtained in this work can be reproduced from this Github repository: https://github.com/safouaneelg/SyMTRS.

item.title: iGeo-LLM: Identification of geological features using multi-source data based on LLM during shield tunnelling
item.dc:creator: Tao Yan
Shui-Long Shen
Zhen-Yu Yin
Ning Zhang
Hao-Ruo Xu
item.description: Geological information is essential to ensure the safety and efficiency of shield tunnelling. However, the concealed excavation environment limits direct observation of geological features and therefore increases construction risk during tunnelling. This study proposes a framework for identification of geological features using multi-source data and large language model (iGeo-LLM) during shield tunnelling. The multi-source data, including shield operation data (SD), muck image data (ID), and vibration data (VD), were analysed by ChatGPT model to extract their features and generate the structured data, thereby facilitating effective understanding by LLMs under a few-shot prompt learning paradigm. The retrieval-augmented generation flow (RAGFlow) was then incorporated to enhance output reliability and improve the factual grounding of LLM inference by introducing project-specific structured knowledge. The external knowledge base in RAGFlow was constructed using different proportions of multi-source data (hereafter called RAG data). Prompt engineering was further employed to constrain evidence-based output generation, and improve the interpretability and reliability. Results show that both RAG enhancement and multi-source data fusion markedly improve the performance of LLM-based geological-feature identification. The proposed iGeo-LLM achieves its best performance under the integrated SD, ID, and VD setting. The main advantage of the proposed framework lies in retrieval-augmented reasoning over heterogeneous tunnelling data, while conventional machine learning models remain highly effective on single-source datasets. A case study in Guangzhou demonstrates the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed multi-source RAG-enhanced framework for geological interpretation during shield tunnelling. Published: 2026-04-26T14:46:14+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.772 (must_read)

Relevance: 0.772 (must_read)

Geological information is essential to ensure the safety and efficiency of shield tunnelling. However, the concealed excavation environment limits direct observation of geological features and therefore increases construction risk during tunnelling. This study proposes a framework for identification of geological features using multi-source data and large language model (iGeo-LLM) during shield tunnelling. The multi-source data, including shield operation data (SD), muck image data (ID), and vibration data (VD), were analysed by ChatGPT model to extract their features and generate the structured data, thereby facilitating effective understanding by LLMs under a few-shot prompt learning paradigm. The retrieval-augmented generation flow (RAGFlow) was then incorporated to enhance output reliability and improve the factual grounding of LLM inference by introducing project-specific structured knowledge. The external knowledge base in RAGFlow was constructed using different proportions of multi-source data (hereafter called RAG data). Prompt engineering was further employed to constrain evidence-based output generation, and improve the interpretability and reliability. Results show that both RAG enhancement and multi-source data fusion markedly improve the performance of LLM-based geological-feature identification. The proposed iGeo-LLM achieves its best performance under the integrated SD, ID, and VD setting. The main advantage of the proposed framework lies in retrieval-augmented reasoning over heterogeneous tunnelling data, while conventional machine learning models remain highly effective on single-source datasets. A case study in Guangzhou demonstrates the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed multi-source RAG-enhanced framework for geological interpretation during shield tunnelling.

item.title: Unlocking the Power of Critical Factors for 3D Visual Geometry Estimation
item.dc:creator: Guangkai Xu
Hua Geng
Huanyi Zheng
Songyi Yin
Yanlong Sun
Hao Chen
Chunhua Shen
item.description: Feed-forward visual geometry estimation has recently made rapid progress. However, an important gap remains: multi-frame models usually produce better cross-frame consistency, yet they often underperform strong per-frame methods on single-frame accuracy. This observation motivates our systematic investigation into the critical factors driving model performance through rigorous ablation studies, which reveals several key insights: 1) Scaling up data diversity and quality unlocks further performance gains even in state-of-the-art visual geometry estimation methods; 2) Commonly adopted confidence-aware loss and gradient-based loss mechanisms may unintentionally hinder performance; 3) Joint supervision through both per-sequence and per-frame alignment improves results, while local region alignment surprisingly degrades performance. Furthermore, we introduce two enhancements to integrate the advantages of optimization-based methods and high-resolution inputs: a consistency loss function that enforces alignment between depth maps, camera parameters, and point maps, and an efficient architectural design that leverages high-resolution information. We integrate these designs into CARVE, a resolution-enhanced model for feed-forward visual geometry estimation. Experiments on point cloud reconstruction, video depth estimation, and camera pose/intrinsic estimation show that CARVE achieves strong and robust performance across diverse benchmarks. Published: 2026-04-23T14:20:44+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.771 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Guangkai Xu; Hua Geng; Huanyi Zheng; Songyi Yin; Yanlong Sun; Hao Chen; Chunhua Shen

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.771 (must_read)

Feed-forward visual geometry estimation has recently made rapid progress. However, an important gap remains: multi-frame models usually produce better cross-frame consistency, yet they often underperform strong per-frame methods on single-frame accuracy. This observation motivates our systematic investigation into the critical factors driving model performance through rigorous ablation studies, which reveals several key insights: 1) Scaling up data diversity and quality unlocks further performance gains even in state-of-the-art visual geometry estimation methods; 2) Commonly adopted confidence-aware loss and gradient-based loss mechanisms may unintentionally hinder performance; 3) Joint supervision through both per-sequence and per-frame alignment improves results, while local region alignment surprisingly degrades performance. Furthermore, we introduce two enhancements to integrate the advantages of optimization-based methods and high-resolution inputs: a consistency loss function that enforces alignment between depth maps, camera parameters, and point maps, and an efficient architectural design that leverages high-resolution information. We integrate these designs into CARVE, a resolution-enhanced model for feed-forward visual geometry estimation. Experiments on point cloud reconstruction, video depth estimation, and camera pose/intrinsic estimation show that CARVE achieves strong and robust performance across diverse benchmarks.

item.title: An Interpretable Hybrid-Domain Fusion Network for Subjective Memory Concern Diagnosis
item.dc:creator: Jinyu Wen
Guanhua Zhang
Jiying Liu
Meie Fang
Pengfei Zhang
Weiping Ding
Herwig Unger
item.description: The neurodegeneration in Alzheimer’s Disease (AD) is irreversible. Early precise screening and intervention are critical to slowing disease progression. Subjective Memory Concern (SMC), recognized as the preclinical stage of AD, exhibits specific yet extremely subtle structural changes in the brain that are difficult to detect effectively using traditional methods. Current artificial intelligence-based research on SMC identification remains in its preliminary exploratory phase, with existing methods facing limitations such as restricted receptive fields and weak interpretability. To address these challenges, this paper proposes an I nterpretable Hybrid-Domain Fusion Net work for SMC Diagnosis (SMC-INet). The proposed model primarily consists of two modules: the Frequency-Domain Attention Enhancement Module (FDAEM) and the Spatio-Temporal Aware Fusion Module (STAFM). FDAEM innovatively introduces frequency-domain attention mechanisms into SMC diagnosis. By combining frequency and spatial information, it effectively captures subtle brain structural changes associated with SMC and extracts discriminative instance-level features. To the best of our knowledge, this work pioneers the application of frequency-domain analysis to intelligent SMC diagnosis. STAFM employs state-space models to dynamically model inter-instance relationships, enabling targeted screening and integration of key pathological features to enhance discrimination between SMC and other states. Additionally, this paper improves the interpretability of the decision-making process by visualizing feature interaction weights, revealing the specific contributions of key brain regions to classification decisions. Extensive cross-validation demonstrates SMC-INet provides highly competitive and balanced diagnostic performance, overcoming the severe sensitivity-specificity trade-offs seen in state-of-the-art baselines. Demonstrating unprecedented peak potential, the model uniquely surpasses 91% across all key metrics in its best-performing fold. These results highlight its strong potential to serve as an effective computer-aided diagnosis (CAD) tool to assist radiologists in early AD screening. Published: 2026-04-25T15:10:36+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.768 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jinyu Wen; Guanhua Zhang; Jiying Liu; Meie Fang; Pengfei Zhang; Weiping Ding; Herwig Unger

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104395

Relevance: 0.768 (must_read)

The neurodegeneration in Alzheimer’s Disease (AD) is irreversible. Early precise screening and intervention are critical to slowing disease progression. Subjective Memory Concern (SMC), recognized as the preclinical stage of AD, exhibits specific yet extremely subtle structural changes in the brain that are difficult to detect effectively using traditional methods. Current artificial intelligence-based research on SMC identification remains in its preliminary exploratory phase, with existing methods facing limitations such as restricted receptive fields and weak interpretability. To address these challenges, this paper proposes an I nterpretable Hybrid-Domain Fusion Net work for SMC Diagnosis (SMC-INet). The proposed model primarily consists of two modules: the Frequency-Domain Attention Enhancement Module (FDAEM) and the Spatio-Temporal Aware Fusion Module (STAFM). FDAEM innovatively introduces frequency-domain attention mechanisms into SMC diagnosis. By combining frequency and spatial information, it effectively captures subtle brain structural changes associated with SMC and extracts discriminative instance-level features. To the best of our knowledge, this work pioneers the application of frequency-domain analysis to intelligent SMC diagnosis. STAFM employs state-space models to dynamically model inter-instance relationships, enabling targeted screening and integration of key pathological features to enhance discrimination between SMC and other states. Additionally, this paper improves the interpretability of the decision-making process by visualizing feature interaction weights, revealing the specific contributions of key brain regions to classification decisions. Extensive cross-validation demonstrates SMC-INet provides highly competitive and balanced diagnostic performance, overcoming the severe sensitivity-specificity trade-offs seen in state-of-the-art baselines. Demonstrating unprecedented peak potential, the model uniquely surpasses 91% across all key metrics in its best-performing fold. These results highlight its strong potential to serve as an effective computer-aided diagnosis (CAD) tool to assist radiologists in early AD screening.

item.title: Adapting Vision-Language Foundation Model for Next Generation Medical Ultrasound Image Analysis
item.dc:creator: Jingguo Qu
Xinyang Han
Jia Ai
Juan Wu
Tong Zhao
Tonghuan Xiao
Sheng Ning
Yuqi Yang
Jing Qin
Ann Dorothy King
Winnie Chiu-Wing Chu
Jing Cai
Michael Tin-Cheung Ying
item.description: Vision-Language Foundation Models (VLFMs) exhibit remarkable generalization, yet their direct application to medical ultrasound is severely hindered by a profound modality gap. The unique acoustic physics of ultrasound, characterized by speckle noise, shadowing, and heterogeneous textures, often degrades the performance of off-the-shelf VLFMs. To bridge this gap, we propose a novel Hybrid Tuning (HT) strategy for the parameter-efficient adaptation of CLIP-based models to ultrasound analysis. Instead of updating the pre-trained weights, HT freezes the visual backbone and integrates a specialized lightweight adapter. This adapter features a Frequency Filtering module to suppress domain-specific periodic artifacts and a Noise Estimation module to dynamically calibrate feature representations. Extensive evaluations across six multi-center datasets demonstrate that our HT-enhanced models significantly outperform existing state-of-the-art adapters and medical VLFMs in both segmentation and classification tasks. Notably, HT exhibits exceptional data efficiency in few-shot scenarios and robust cross-dataset generalization. Our findings prove that preserving pre-trained semantic priors while explicitly modeling ultrasound-specific noise is key to unlocking foundational intelligence in automated ultrasound diagnosis. The source code will be made publicly available. Published: 2026-04-25T23:57:20+00:00 Venue: Expert Systems with Applications Score: 0.768 (must_read)
