Authors: Jingguo Qu; Xinyang Han; Jia Ai; Juan Wu; Tong Zhao; Tonghuan Xiao; Sheng Ning; Yuqi Yang; Jing Qin; Ann Dorothy King; Winnie Chiu-Wing Chu; Jing Cai; Michael Tin-Cheung Ying

Journal: Expert Systems with Applications

DOI: 10.1016/j.eswa.2026.132560

Relevance: 0.768 (must_read)

Vision-Language Foundation Models (VLFMs) exhibit remarkable generalization, yet their direct application to medical ultrasound is severely hindered by a profound modality gap. The unique acoustic physics of ultrasound, characterized by speckle noise, shadowing, and heterogeneous textures, often degrades the performance of off-the-shelf VLFMs. To bridge this gap, we propose a novel Hybrid Tuning (HT) strategy for the parameter-efficient adaptation of CLIP-based models to ultrasound analysis. Instead of updating the pre-trained weights, HT freezes the visual backbone and integrates a specialized lightweight adapter. This adapter features a Frequency Filtering module to suppress domain-specific periodic artifacts and a Noise Estimation module to dynamically calibrate feature representations. Extensive evaluations across six multi-center datasets demonstrate that our HT-enhanced models significantly outperform existing state-of-the-art adapters and medical VLFMs in both segmentation and classification tasks. Notably, HT exhibits exceptional data efficiency in few-shot scenarios and robust cross-dataset generalization. Our findings prove that preserving pre-trained semantic priors while explicitly modeling ultrasound-specific noise is key to unlocking foundational intelligence in automated ultrasound diagnosis. The source code will be made publicly available.

item.title: Mitigating Clever Hans Strategies in Image Classifiers through Generating Counterexamples
item.dc:creator: Sidney Bender
Ole Delzer
Jan Herrmann
Heike Antje Marxfeld
Klaus-Robert Müller
Grégoire Montavon
item.description: Deep learning models remain vulnerable to spurious correlations , leading to so-called Clever Hans predictors that undermine robustness even in large-scale foundation and self-supervised models. Group distributional robustness methods, such as Deep Feature Reweighting (DFR) rely on explicit group labels to upweight underrepresented subgroups, but face key limitations: (1) group labels are often unavailable, (2) low within-group sample sizes hinder coverage of the subgroup distribution, and (3) performance degrades sharply when multiple spurious correlations fragment the data into even smaller groups. We propose Counterfactual Knowledge Distillation (CFKD), a framework that sidesteps these issues by generating diverse counterfactuals, enabling a human annotator to efficiently explore and correct the model’s decision boundaries through a knowledge distillation step. Unlike DFR, our method not only reweights the undersampled groups, but it also enriches them with new data points. Our method does not require any confounder labels, achieves effective scaling to multiple confounders, and yields balanced generalization across groups. We demonstrate CFKD’s efficacy across five datasets, spanning synthetic tasks to an industrial application, with particularly strong gains in low-data regimes with pronounced spurious correlations. Additionally, we provide an ablation study on the effect of the chosen counterfactual explainer and teacher model, highlighting their impact on robustness. Published: 2026-04-25T15:10:50+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.768 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Sidney Bender; Ole Delzer; Jan Herrmann; Heike Antje Marxfeld; Klaus-Robert Müller; Grégoire Montavon

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104406

Relevance: 0.768 (consider)

Deep learning models remain vulnerable to spurious correlations , leading to so-called Clever Hans predictors that undermine robustness even in large-scale foundation and self-supervised models. Group distributional robustness methods, such as Deep Feature Reweighting (DFR) rely on explicit group labels to upweight underrepresented subgroups, but face key limitations: (1) group labels are often unavailable, (2) low within-group sample sizes hinder coverage of the subgroup distribution, and (3) performance degrades sharply when multiple spurious correlations fragment the data into even smaller groups. We propose Counterfactual Knowledge Distillation (CFKD), a framework that sidesteps these issues by generating diverse counterfactuals, enabling a human annotator to efficiently explore and correct the model’s decision boundaries through a knowledge distillation step. Unlike DFR, our method not only reweights the undersampled groups, but it also enriches them with new data points. Our method does not require any confounder labels, achieves effective scaling to multiple confounders, and yields balanced generalization across groups. We demonstrate CFKD’s efficacy across five datasets, spanning synthetic tasks to an industrial application, with particularly strong gains in low-data regimes with pronounced spurious correlations. Additionally, we provide an ablation study on the effect of the chosen counterfactual explainer and teacher model, highlighting their impact on robustness.

item.title: ERIS: An Energy-Guided Feature Disentanglement Framework for Out-of-Distribution Time Series Classification
item.dc:creator: Xin Wu
Fei Teng
Ji Zhang
Ji Zhang
Xingwang Li
Yuxuan Liang
item.description: Achieving reliable time-series classification under non-stationary conditions remains a central challenge in robust information processing. Conventional data-driven models often struggle to separate domain-specific variations from label-relevant semantics, leading to degraded generalization and miscalibrated predictions under distribution shifts. To address this issue, we propose an end-to-end Energy-Regularized Information for Shift-Robustness (ERIS) framework. ERIS introduces a variance-informed disentanglement scheme that uses signal variance as an inductive prior, where learnable energy scores characterize signal compatibility across domains. Based on this design, we develop an energy-guided calibration mechanism that encourages the separation of domain-related variations from class-invariant semantics while improving uncertainty calibration under shift. We further incorporate a weight-level orthogonality regularization to encourage structural decorrelation between parameter subspaces, together with an auxiliary adversarial generalization mechanism that enhances robustness through structured perturbations. Experiments on five large-scale benchmarks show that ERIS achieves strong and consistent performance, reaching an average classification accuracy of 78.38%, outperforming the best-performing baseline overall by 2.29 percentage points, and the lowest macro-averaged expected calibration error (ECE) of 0.12, reducing the error by 0.05 compared with the best energy-based baseline. To support reproducibility, the source code is publicly available at https://github.com/wuliwuxin/ERIS . Published: 2026-04-26T05:58:46+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.768 (consider)

Authors: Xin Wu; Fei Teng; Ji Zhang; Ji Zhang; Xingwang Li; Yuxuan Liang

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104407

Relevance: 0.768 (consider)

Achieving reliable time-series classification under non-stationary conditions remains a central challenge in robust information processing. Conventional data-driven models often struggle to separate domain-specific variations from label-relevant semantics, leading to degraded generalization and miscalibrated predictions under distribution shifts. To address this issue, we propose an end-to-end Energy-Regularized Information for Shift-Robustness (ERIS) framework. ERIS introduces a variance-informed disentanglement scheme that uses signal variance as an inductive prior, where learnable energy scores characterize signal compatibility across domains. Based on this design, we develop an energy-guided calibration mechanism that encourages the separation of domain-related variations from class-invariant semantics while improving uncertainty calibration under shift. We further incorporate a weight-level orthogonality regularization to encourage structural decorrelation between parameter subspaces, together with an auxiliary adversarial generalization mechanism that enhances robustness through structured perturbations. Experiments on five large-scale benchmarks show that ERIS achieves strong and consistent performance, reaching an average classification accuracy of 78.38%, outperforming the best-performing baseline overall by 2.29 percentage points, and the lowest macro-averaged expected calibration error (ECE) of 0.12, reducing the error by 0.05 compared with the best energy-based baseline. To support reproducibility, the source code is publicly available at https://github.com/wuliwuxin/ERIS .

item.title: SS3D: End2End Self-Supervised 3D from Web Videos
item.dc:creator: Marwane Hariat
Gianni Franchi
David Filliat
Antoine Manzanera
item.description: We present SS3D, a web-scale SfM-based self-supervision pretraining pipeline for feed-forward 3D estimation from monocular video. Our model jointly predicts depth, ego-motion, and intrinsics in a single forward pass and is trained/evaluated as a coherent end-to-end 3D estimator. To stabilize joint learning, we use an intrinsics-first two-stage schedule and a unified single-checkpoint evaluation protocol. Scaling SfM self-supervision to unconstrained web video is challenging due to weak multi-view observability and strong corpus heterogeneity; we address these with a multi-view signal proxy (MVS) used for filtering and curriculum sampling, and with expert training distilled into a single student. Pretraining on YouTube-8M (~100M frames after filtering) yields strong cross-domain zero-shot transfer and improved fine-tuning performance over prior self-supervised baselines. We release the pretrained checkpoint and code. Published: 2026-04-24T16:12:44+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.766 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Marwane Hariat; Gianni Franchi; David Filliat; Antoine Manzanera

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.766 (consider)

We present SS3D, a web-scale SfM-based self-supervision pretraining pipeline for feed-forward 3D estimation from monocular video. Our model jointly predicts depth, ego-motion, and intrinsics in a single forward pass and is trained/evaluated as a coherent end-to-end 3D estimator. To stabilize joint learning, we use an intrinsics-first two-stage schedule and a unified single-checkpoint evaluation protocol. Scaling SfM self-supervision to unconstrained web video is challenging due to weak multi-view observability and strong corpus heterogeneity; we address these with a multi-view signal proxy (MVS) used for filtering and curriculum sampling, and with expert training distilled into a single student. Pretraining on YouTube-8M (~100M frames after filtering) yields strong cross-domain zero-shot transfer and improved fine-tuning performance over prior self-supervised baselines. We release the pretrained checkpoint and code.

item.title: Zero-shot transfer learning bearing fault diagnosis based on generative adversarial network with multi-attribute vector fusion
item.dc:creator: Bin Wang
Dengke Jiu
Pengfei Liang
Lijie Zhang
item.description: Zero-shot learning (ZSL) has recently emerged as a promising paradigm for intelligent fault diagnosis (FD), enabling the recognition of unseen fault types. However, in practical industrial settings, collecting data across all operating conditions is costly and often infeasible, making cross-condition ZSL a challenging yet valuable task. To address this, a zero-shot transfer learning FD framework based on multi-attribute fusion and generative adversarial learning is proposed. At the semantic level, a high-dimensional attribute space is constructed by integrating textual descriptions, graph relationships, statistical metrics, and operating condition encodings to enhance discriminability and transferability. At the feature level, a dual-channel extractor is developed that combines multi-scale wavelet convolution (WTConv) and fast Fourier transform (FFT) convolution to capture transient impulses and frequency-domain features jointly. A Wasserstein generative adversarial network with gradient penalty (WGAN-GP) is further employed to synthesize discriminative features for unseen classes under attribute constraints, with an auxiliary classifier ensuring semantic consistency. To alleviate domain shift, a prototype adaptation strategy aligns the synthesized prototypes with target domain distributions during inference. Experimental results on three public datasets demonstrate that the proposed method consistently exhibits strong competitiveness and robustness across four cross-condition zero-shot FD tasks, achieving average diagnostic accuracies of 89.03%, 89.02%, and 86.35%, respectively. Compared with conventional zero-shot FD approaches, the proposed method improves accuracy by more than 10%, and further achieves an improvement of approximately 3%-6% over current state-of-the-art methods. Published: 2026-04-26T14:44:17+00:00 Venue: Expert Systems with Applications Score: 0.765 (consider)

Authors: Bin Wang; Dengke Jiu; Pengfei Liang; Lijie Zhang

Journal: Expert Systems with Applications

DOI: 10.1016/j.eswa.2026.132641

Relevance: 0.765 (consider)

Zero-shot learning (ZSL) has recently emerged as a promising paradigm for intelligent fault diagnosis (FD), enabling the recognition of unseen fault types. However, in practical industrial settings, collecting data across all operating conditions is costly and often infeasible, making cross-condition ZSL a challenging yet valuable task. To address this, a zero-shot transfer learning FD framework based on multi-attribute fusion and generative adversarial learning is proposed. At the semantic level, a high-dimensional attribute space is constructed by integrating textual descriptions, graph relationships, statistical metrics, and operating condition encodings to enhance discriminability and transferability. At the feature level, a dual-channel extractor is developed that combines multi-scale wavelet convolution (WTConv) and fast Fourier transform (FFT) convolution to capture transient impulses and frequency-domain features jointly. A Wasserstein generative adversarial network with gradient penalty (WGAN-GP) is further employed to synthesize discriminative features for unseen classes under attribute constraints, with an auxiliary classifier ensuring semantic consistency. To alleviate domain shift, a prototype adaptation strategy aligns the synthesized prototypes with target domain distributions during inference. Experimental results on three public datasets demonstrate that the proposed method consistently exhibits strong competitiveness and robustness across four cross-condition zero-shot FD tasks, achieving average diagnostic accuracies of 89.03%, 89.02%, and 86.35%, respectively. Compared with conventional zero-shot FD approaches, the proposed method improves accuracy by more than 10%, and further achieves an improvement of approximately 3%-6% over current state-of-the-art methods.

item.title: Context Unrolling in Omni Models
item.dc:creator: Ceyuan Yang
Zhijie Lin
Yang Zhao
Fei Xiao
Hao He
Qi Zhao
Chaorui Deng
Kunchang Li
Zihan Ding
Yuwei Guo
Fuyun Wang
Fangqi Zhu
Xiaonan Nie
Shenhan Zhu
Shanchuan Lin
Hongsheng Li
Weilin Huang
Guang Shi
Haoqi Fan
item.description: We present Omni, a unified multimodal model natively trained on diverse modalities, including text, images, videos, 3D geometry, and hidden representations. We find that such training enables Context Unrolling, where the model explicitly reasons across multiple modal representations before producing predictions. This process enables the model to aggregate complementary information across heterogeneous modalities, facilitating a more faithful approximation of the shared multimodal knowledge manifold and improving downstream reasoning fidelity. As a result, Omni achieves strong performance on both multimodal generation and understanding benchmarks, while demonstrating advanced multimodal reasoning capabilities, including in-context generation of text, image, video, and 3D geometry. Published: 2026-04-23T17:58:38+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.765 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ceyuan Yang; Zhijie Lin; Yang Zhao; Fei Xiao; Hao He; Qi Zhao; Chaorui Deng; Kunchang Li; Zihan Ding; Yuwei Guo; Fuyun Wang; Fangqi Zhu; Xiaonan Nie; Shenhan Zhu; Shanchuan Lin; Hongsheng Li; Weilin Huang; Guang Shi; Haoqi Fan

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.765 (consider)

We present Omni, a unified multimodal model natively trained on diverse modalities, including text, images, videos, 3D geometry, and hidden representations. We find that such training enables Context Unrolling, where the model explicitly reasons across multiple modal representations before producing predictions. This process enables the model to aggregate complementary information across heterogeneous modalities, facilitating a more faithful approximation of the shared multimodal knowledge manifold and improving downstream reasoning fidelity. As a result, Omni achieves strong performance on both multimodal generation and understanding benchmarks, while demonstrating advanced multimodal reasoning capabilities, including in-context generation of text, image, video, and 3D geometry.

item.title: Mean teacher with pseudo-label correction for cross-domain image classification
item.dc:creator: Yanting Pei
Fan Yang
item.description: Cross-domain image classification often suffers from the domain gap between source domain with labels and target domain without labels. Unsupervised domain adaptation is employed to address this issue, one of the typical frameworks of which is the teacher-student model based method. They have a large accuracy gain in cross-domain image classification, where the student model is supervised by the pseudo-labels from the teacher model. However, the pseudo-labels generated by the teacher model may have lower quality, that is, contain noisy labels, which leads to sub-optimal classification performance in target domain. To address this problem, we propose a Mean Teacher with Pseudo-label Correction (MTPC) for cross-domain image classification. Our goal is to improve the quality of the pseudo-labels and thereby boost classification performance in the target domain. To achieve this goal, we propose a Pseudo-label Correction Module (PCM) to select and correct pseudo-labels and then classify them, instead of using pseudo-labels directly for final classification. Specifically, the teacher model is first used to generate pseudo-labels for the target domain data. Subsequently, the original target domain images, the masked images generated from the target domain, and the pseudo-labels are all fed together into the pseudo-label correction module. This module then uses a probability threshold to identify and dynamically correct labels that may have noise. PCM can be integrated into various unsupervised domain adaptation image classification methods. We conduct a large number of experiments on widely used cross-domain image classification datasets VisDA-2017, Office-Home and Office-31, and the experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of our method. Published: 2026-04-25T15:41:29+00:00 Venue: Neurocomputing Score: 0.764 (consider)

Authors: Yanting Pei; Fan Yang

Journal: Neurocomputing

DOI: 10.1016/j.neucom.2026.133670

Relevance: 0.764 (consider)

Cross-domain image classification often suffers from the domain gap between source domain with labels and target domain without labels. Unsupervised domain adaptation is employed to address this issue, one of the typical frameworks of which is the teacher-student model based method. They have a large accuracy gain in cross-domain image classification, where the student model is supervised by the pseudo-labels from the teacher model. However, the pseudo-labels generated by the teacher model may have lower quality, that is, contain noisy labels, which leads to sub-optimal classification performance in target domain. To address this problem, we propose a Mean Teacher with Pseudo-label Correction (MTPC) for cross-domain image classification. Our goal is to improve the quality of the pseudo-labels and thereby boost classification performance in the target domain. To achieve this goal, we propose a Pseudo-label Correction Module (PCM) to select and correct pseudo-labels and then classify them, instead of using pseudo-labels directly for final classification. Specifically, the teacher model is first used to generate pseudo-labels for the target domain data. Subsequently, the original target domain images, the masked images generated from the target domain, and the pseudo-labels are all fed together into the pseudo-label correction module. This module then uses a probability threshold to identify and dynamically correct labels that may have noise. PCM can be integrated into various unsupervised domain adaptation image classification methods. We conduct a large number of experiments on widely used cross-domain image classification datasets VisDA-2017, Office-Home and Office-31, and the experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of our method.

item.title: PACT: Phase-amplitude collaboration transformer for lightweight image super-resolution
item.dc:creator: Kexin Liu
Lulu Pan
Jiangjun Peng
Yani Zhu
Ke Xu
Yiqin Wang
item.description: Although Vision Transformer (ViT) has demonstrated excellent performance in single image super-resolution (SISR) tasks, the global modeling nature of its self-attention mechanism often leads to over-smoothing of high-frequency details. Moreover, its inherent high computational complexity poses challenges in balancing model capacity and reconstruction accuracy. To address these issues, this paper proposes a Phase-Amplitude Collaboration Transformer (PACT), aiming to provide an efficient solution for SISR in resource-constrained scenarios. First, we design a Phase-Amplitude Collaboration and Modulation Block (PACMB), which enables more accurate extraction of high-frequency details through a two-stage process of phase-amplitude collaborative alignment and frequency-domain feature modulation. Second, an Efficient Adaptive Aggregation Block (EAAB) is proposed, based on a lightweight dual-branch attention mechanism, to achieve adaptive weighted aggregation of spatial and frequency domain features, thereby further enhancing feature representation capability. Finally, by integrating the high-frequency feature extraction advantage of PACMB and the dual-domain feature aggregation capability of EAAB, an efficient phase-amplitude collaboration Transformer architecture is constructed. To accommodate different resource constraints, we further design three lightweight versions (Tiny, PACT, and Large), achieving a balance between flexibility and practicality. Extensive experimental results show that all three versions of PACT achieve state-of-the-art performance on public benchmark datasets. Specifically, in the " role="presentation"> 4 super-resolution task on the Urban100 dataset, each version achieves a PSNR gain of up to 0.43–0.45 dB compared to state-of-the-art models of similar complexity. Additionally, extended experiments applying PACT to image classification and real-world SR tasks further validate its superior performance and generalization capability. Published: 2026-04-25T06:12:55+00:00 Venue: Neurocomputing Score: 0.764 (consider)

Although Vision Transformer (ViT) has demonstrated excellent performance in single image super-resolution (SISR) tasks, the global modeling nature of its self-attention mechanism often leads to over-smoothing of high-frequency details. Moreover, its inherent high computational complexity poses challenges in balancing model capacity and reconstruction accuracy. To address these issues, this paper proposes a Phase-Amplitude Collaboration Transformer (PACT), aiming to provide an efficient solution for SISR in resource-constrained scenarios. First, we design a Phase-Amplitude Collaboration and Modulation Block (PACMB), which enables more accurate extraction of high-frequency details through a two-stage process of phase-amplitude collaborative alignment and frequency-domain feature modulation. Second, an Efficient Adaptive Aggregation Block (EAAB) is proposed, based on a lightweight dual-branch attention mechanism, to achieve adaptive weighted aggregation of spatial and frequency domain features, thereby further enhancing feature representation capability. Finally, by integrating the high-frequency feature extraction advantage of PACMB and the dual-domain feature aggregation capability of EAAB, an efficient phase-amplitude collaboration Transformer architecture is constructed. To accommodate different resource constraints, we further design three lightweight versions (Tiny, PACT, and Large), achieving a balance between flexibility and practicality. Extensive experimental results show that all three versions of PACT achieve state-of-the-art performance on public benchmark datasets. Specifically, in the " role="presentation"> 4 super-resolution task on the Urban100 dataset, each version achieves a PSNR gain of up to 0.43–0.45 dB compared to state-of-the-art models of similar complexity. Additionally, extended experiments applying PACT to image classification and real-world SR tasks further validate its superior performance and generalization capability.

item.title: ATDFusion: Adapter-Tuned Dual-Branch Network for multimodal medical image fusion
item.dc:creator: Chenyang Li
Rui Zhu
Hang Zhao
Xiongfei Li
Xiaoli Zhang
item.description: Multimodal medical image fusion aims to combine complementary visual information from different imaging modalities to assist diagnosis and clinical decision-making. Existing methods often struggle to balance global semantic representation and local detail preservation, leading to blurred or incomplete features in salient regions. This work proposes ATDFusion (Adapter-Tuned Dual-Branch Network), featuring a global semantic branch and an auxiliary detail branch to jointly capture high-level context and fine-grained details. A Low-Rank Dynamic Token Adapter (LR-DTA) adaptively fine-tunes intermediate layers of pretrained models based on token number and rank. Additionally, a Fusion Enhancement Guidance (FEG) module imposes explicit spatial supervision via a saliency-aware loss to strictly preserve diagnostically critical regions. On three typical tasks (MRI-CT, MRI-PET, MRI-SPECT), ATDFusion surpasses state-of-the-art methods, improving SSIM and CC by 11.93% and 5.07%, respectively. Other metrics (e.g., EN, PSNR) also achieve leading results, validating its effectiveness. Furthermore, the model demonstrates strong zero-shot generalization on the unseen HECKTOR 2025 dataset. Code is available at https://github.com/pluto628/ATDFusion . Published: 2026-04-26T14:38:32+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.763 (consider)

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.113848

Relevance: 0.763 (consider)

Multimodal medical image fusion aims to combine complementary visual information from different imaging modalities to assist diagnosis and clinical decision-making. Existing methods often struggle to balance global semantic representation and local detail preservation, leading to blurred or incomplete features in salient regions. This work proposes ATDFusion (Adapter-Tuned Dual-Branch Network), featuring a global semantic branch and an auxiliary detail branch to jointly capture high-level context and fine-grained details. A Low-Rank Dynamic Token Adapter (LR-DTA) adaptively fine-tunes intermediate layers of pretrained models based on token number and rank. Additionally, a Fusion Enhancement Guidance (FEG) module imposes explicit spatial supervision via a saliency-aware loss to strictly preserve diagnostically critical regions. On three typical tasks (MRI-CT, MRI-PET, MRI-SPECT), ATDFusion surpasses state-of-the-art methods, improving SSIM and CC by 11.93% and 5.07%, respectively. Other metrics (e.g., EN, PSNR) also achieve leading results, validating its effectiveness. Furthermore, the model demonstrates strong zero-shot generalization on the unseen HECKTOR 2025 dataset. Code is available at https://github.com/pluto628/ATDFusion .

item.title: Sapiens2
item.dc:creator: Rawal Khirodkar
He Wen
Julieta Martinez
Yuan Dong
Su Zhaoen
Shunsuke Saito
item.description: We present Sapiens2, a model family of high-resolution transformers for human-centric vision focused on generalization, versatility, and high-fidelity outputs. Our model sizes range from 0.4 to 5 billion parameters, with native 1K resolution and hierarchical variants that support 4K. Sapiens2 substantially improves over its predecessor in both pretraining and post-training. First, to learn features that capture low-level details (for dense prediction) and high-level semantics (for zero-shot or few-label settings), we combine masked image reconstruction with self-distilled contrastive objectives. Our evaluations show that this unified pretraining objective is better suited for a wider range of downstream tasks. Second, along the data axis, we pretrain on a curated dataset of 1 billion high-quality human images and improve the quality and quantity of task annotations. Third, architecturally, we incorporate advances from frontier models that enable longer training schedules with improved stability. Our 4K models adopt windowed attention to reason over longer spatial context and are pretrained with 2K output resolution. Sapiens2 sets a new state-of-the-art and improves over the first generation on pose (+4 mAP), body-part segmentation (+24.3 mIoU), normal estimation (45.6% lower angular error) and extends to new tasks such as pointmap and albedo estimation. Code: https://github.com/facebookresearch/sapiens2 Published: 2026-04-23T13:45:32+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.762 (consider)

Second, along the data axis, we pretrain on a curated dataset of 1 billion high-quality human images and improve the quality and quantity of task annotations.
